  have h_imp : ctble U → ∃ (f : U → Nat), one_to_one f := by
    intro h
    have h₁ : U ≈ Set.univ := by
      simpa [Set.univ] using h
    -- Use the fact that U is countable to find an injective function to the natural numbers
    have h₂ : ∃ (f : U → ℕ), Function.Injective f := by
      -- Use the equivalence to the set of natural numbers to construct an injective function
      have h₃ : Nonempty (U ≃ ℕ) := by
        -- Use the countability of U to get an equivalence to ℕ
        have h₄ : Countable U := by simpa using h
        -- Extract the equivalence from the countability condition
        exact h₄.nonempty
      -- Obtain the equivalence and use it to construct the injective function
      obtain ⟨e⟩ := h₃
      refine' ⟨fun x => e x, _⟩
      -- Prove that the function is injective using the properties of equivalence
      intro x₁ x₂ h₅
      have h₆ := congr_arg (fun x => e.symm x) h₅
      simp at h₆
      <;> simp_all [Equiv.injective]
    -- Obtain the injective function and use it to complete the proof
    obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h₂
    refine' ⟨f, _⟩
    -- Prove that the function is one-to-one
    intro x y hxy
    apply hf
    simp_all
  
  have h_converse : (∃ (f : U → Nat), one_to_one f) → ctble U := by
    intro h
    have h₁ : ∃ (f : U → ℕ), Function.Injective f := by
      obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h
      refine' ⟨f, _⟩
      intro x y hxy
      apply hf
      <;> simp_all [Function.Injective]
    -- Use the fact that there exists an injective function to ℕ to show U is countable
    have h₂ : Countable U := by
      obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h₁
      -- Use the injective function to show U is countable
      exact Countable.of_injective f hf
    -- Convert the countability of U to the required form
    simpa [Set.univ] using h₂
  
  constructor
  · -- Prove the forward direction: if U is countable, then there exists an injective function from U to ℕ
    intro h
    exact h_imp h
  · -- Prove the reverse direction: if there exists an injective function from U to ℕ, then U is countable
    intro h
    exact h_converse h

  have h₁ : ctble U → ∃ (f : U → Nat), one_to_one f := by
    intro h
    classical
    -- Use the fact that U is countable to get an injection to Nat
    obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h
    -- Use the injection to construct the desired function
    refine' ⟨fun u => f u, _⟩
    intro u v huv
    -- Use the injectivity of the bijection to prove the injectivity of the function
    have h₂ := hf.injOn
    have h₃ : f u = f v := huv
    have h₄ : u = v := by
      apply hf.injective
      exact h₃
    exact h₄
  
  have h₂ : (∃ (f : U → Nat), one_to_one f) → ctble U := by
    intro h
    classical
    -- Obtain the injection from U to ℕ
    obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h
    -- Use the fact that the image of U under f is countable to show U is countable
    have h₃ : ctble U := by
      -- Use the fact that the image of U under f is countable to show U is countable
      have h₄ : ctble (Set.range f) := by
        -- The range of f is a subset of ℕ, hence countable
        exact Set.countable_range f
      -- Since f is injective, U is in bijection with its image under f
      have h₅ : U ≃ Set.range f := by
        -- Define the bijection between U and the range of f
        refine' ⟨fun u => ⟨f u, Set.mem_range_self u⟩, fun x y hxy => hf (by simp_all [Subtype.ext_iff]), fun y => _, fun x => rfl⟩
        -- For any element in the range of f, find its preimage in U
        obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := y
        refine' ⟨x, _⟩
        <;> simp_all [Subtype.ext_iff]
      -- Since the range of f is countable, U is countable
      exact ctble.congr (Equiv.which h₅)
    exact h₃
  -- Combine the two implications to get the final result
  exact ⟨h₁, h₂⟩
